questions.q: The happens-before relationship defines ordering between memory operations. If operation A happens-before operation B, which statement is correct?
options: A's memory effects are guaranteed to be visible to B if they operate on the same variable
A and B execute sequentially, and all memory operations by A are visible before B begins
A executes before B in wall-clock time, but visibility of memory effects is not guaranteed
A's memory effects are guaranteed visible to B before B's results become visible to other threads
questions.explanation: Happens-before is a memory model relationship, not wall-clock time. If A happens-before B, all memory writes by A are visible to B before B reads them. This is stronger than just timing: even if B executes slightly later in real time, the guarantee is that visibility is established. Synchronization primitives (locks, atomics) create happens-before edges.

questions.q: In a sequentially consistent memory model, all memory operations appear to execute in a total global order. If thread A writes X=1 then Y=2, and thread B reads Y=2 then reads X, what must B observe for X?
options: B must read X=1; reads must be consistent with the global order
B might read X=0 because sequential consistency only applies within each thread
B's read of X is undefined if a memory barrier is not used
B could read any value of X because sequential consistency does not apply to reads
questions.explanation: Under sequential consistency, all operations occur in a total order visible to all threads. If B reads Y=2 (which A wrote after X=1), then B's subsequent read of X must see X=1, because X's write precedes Y's write in the total order. Sequential consistency is the strictest model but is expensive to implement; most systems use relaxed models.

questions.q: A memory barrier (or fence) prevents the CPU from reordering memory operations across the barrier. A store-load barrier specifically prevents which reordering?
options: A store before the barrier from moving after a load after the barrier
A load before the barrier from moving after a store after the barrier
Stores to different variables from being reordered
Loads from the same variable from being reordered
questions.explanation: A store-load barrier (full memory barrier) prevents a store that comes before the barrier from being reordered to after a load that comes after the barrier. This is the most restrictive barrier type. Other barriers (acquire, release) prevent specific subsets of reorderings. The exact barrier types depend on the architecture (x86, ARM, etc.).

questions.q: The data race detector finds two unsynchronized accesses to the same memory location where at least one is a write. Why is a data race a serious bug?
options: The compiler is allowed to assume no concurrent accesses and apply optimizations that break concurrent code
Data races are undefined behavior; the program may crash, produce wrong results, or appear to work correctly unpredictably
A data race always results in a crash or hang, guaranteeing detection
Data races are legal in C++ but illegal in Java
questions.explanation: A data race is undefined behavior. The compiler and CPU are not required to provide any guarantees about behavior. Optimizations might reorder, eliminate, or duplicate operations in unexpected ways. The program might appear to work fine on one system and fail on another. This is why data races must be eliminated through proper synchronization.

questions.q: In a relaxed memory model, a thread's own reads and writes are always ordered (read my own write), but reads and writes by different threads may not be. What does relaxed consistency NOT guarantee?
options: A thread's own operations on the same variable maintain order
Writes become visible to other threads in the same order they were executed by the original thread
Synchronization operations (locks, atomics) create ordering between threads
A write by one thread is guaranteed visible to another thread immediately
questions.explanation: Relaxed consistency does NOT guarantee that writes from thread A appear in order to thread B. Without synchronization, writes may be reordered or buffered. Only synchronization primitives (acquiring a lock, reading an atomic with acquire semantics) establish ordering. A single write by A is also not immediately visible to B without synchronization.

questions.q: An acquire operation (e.g., atomic_load with acquire semantics) synchronizes with all prior release operations on the same variable. What is the effect of acquiring a variable?
options: The acquire operation blocks until all prior stores complete, ensuring a flush of write buffers
The acquire operation makes the effects of all prior releases visible to the acquiring thread before subsequent operations execute
The acquire operation converts the variable to read-only mode for other threads
The acquire operation is a hint to the CPU but does not enforce any memory ordering
questions.explanation: An acquire operation establishes a synchronization point. All memory operations by the releasing thread (before its release) are visible to the acquiring thread after the acquire. This is an asymmetric synchronization: release → acquire creates a happens-before edge. The acquire does not block in the traditional sense; it prevents subsequent operations from being reordered before the acquire.

questions.q: Consider two writes to different memory locations by thread A: write X=1, then write Y=2. Under a relaxed consistency model, can these writes appear to thread B in reverse order (B sees Y=2 before X=1)?
options: No, writes to different variables are never reordered
Yes, without synchronization, the CPU is free to reorder writes and thread B may see them out of order
Yes, but only if B is on the same core as A
No, memory barriers prevent all reorderings of writes
questions.explanation: Under relaxed consistency, writes to different variables can be reordered by the CPU or memory system. Thread B might see Y=2 (buffered and flushed early) before X=1 (still in write buffer). If A needs to ensure order, it must use memory barriers or atomics. This is why relaxed models are faster but require programmer discipline.

questions.q: A compare-and-swap (CAS) operation with acquire semantics makes all previous stores by the CAS thread visible to a thread that reads the updated value. This synchronization is an example of which pattern?
options: Release-acquire synchronization through a lock-free update
Sequentially consistent memory for that one operation only
A full memory barrier at the point of the CAS
Relaxed consistency with no ordering guarantees
questions.explanation: A CAS with acquire semantics, paired with a corresponding release by the previous thread that wrote the location, forms a release-acquire synchronization. The CAS acts as an acquire that synchronizes with the prior release. This allows lock-free algorithms to synchronize without locks. Sequential consistency is stronger than needed.

questions.q: In the x86-64 architecture, stores and loads are generally ordered, but load-load, load-store, and store-store reorderings can occur. This is called which memory model?
options: Sequentially consistent
Relaxed
Sequential release-acquire
Total Store Ordering (TSO)
questions.explanation: x86-64 implements Total Store Ordering (TSO), a relatively strong model. Stores are global (released in order to other cores) and a core sees its own stores before other cores' stores. Loads can be reordered with later stores and with each other, but stores are generally ordered. This is stronger than a fully relaxed model (like ARM) but weaker than sequential consistency.

questions.q: A programmer observes a value inconsistency in a multi-threaded program: thread A's write appears to thread B, but not to thread C, even though both B and C check the value at similar times. What is the most likely cause?
options: A buffer overflow in thread A corrupted the memory location
Thread B and C have different memory models due to compiler settings
The program has a data race, and the lack of synchronization allows B and C to observe different values due to caching and memory ordering
The operating system is preempting threads unfairly
questions.explanation: Without synchronization, memory effects of A are not guaranteed to propagate to B and C in the same order or at the same time. B might read the value from A's local cache or a faster path, while C reads a stale value. This is a classic symptom of unsynchronized access to shared memory. Synchronization (locks, atomics) ensures all threads see consistent values relative to the synchronization point.
